selected_abstract:
acquisition of semantic classes for adjectives from distributional evidence
in this paper , we present a clustering experiment directed at the acquisition of semantic classes for adjectives in catalan , using only shallow distributional features . we define a broad-coverage classification for adjectives based on ontological semantics . we classify along two parameters ( number of arguments and ontological kind of denotation ) , achieving reliable agreement results among human judges . the clustering procedure achieves a comparable agreement score for one of the parameters , and a little lower for the other .
human_abstract:
We’re trying to group adjectivs into meaningful buckets for Catalan, but doing it the easy way: just using shallow distro features (basically, what words show up around them) instead of heavy, deep parsing stuff. First we made a broad label set based on ontologial semantics so it actually covers lots of cases, not just toy examples.
Then we sort each adjective along two knobs: (1) how many arguements it tends to take, and (2) what kind of thing it denoates (its ontologial kind). Humans could agree on these labels pretty reliably not perfect, but good enuf. Our clustering method gets about the same agreeemnt for one knob, and a bit lower for the other, so it’s kinda close to people, just not fully there yet. Overall, simple signals go a long way, even if the second part still needs some polishng.

human-2 from Claude AI questions: "We investigate the acquisition of semantic classes for Catalan adjectives using shallow distributional features. The study introduces a broad-coverage classification of adjectives grounded in ontological semantics in order to capture a wide range of linguistic cases. Each adjective is classified according to two parameters: the number of arguments it takes and the ontological type of its denotation. Human annotators show reliable agreement in applying these classifications. A clustering approach based solely on shallow distributional evidence is then used to induce these classes automatically. The resulting clusters achieve agreement comparable to human judgments for one parameter and slightly lower agreement for the other, indicating that simple distributional signals can capture important aspects of adjective semantics while leaving room for further improvement."

selected_abstract: we present a sequential labeling approach to hedge cue detection submitted to the biological portion of task 1 for the conll2010 shared task . our main approach is as follows . we make use of partial syntactic information together with features obtained from the unlabeled corpus , and convert the task into one of sequential biotagging . if a cue is found , a sentence is classified as uncertain and certain otherwise . to examine a large number of feature combinations , we employ a genetic algorithm . while some features obtained by this method are difficult to interpret , they were shown to improve the performance of the final system .
human_abstract: We worked on spotting hedge cues the little words/phrases that make a claim sound less sure in bio text for the CoNLL-2010 shared task (task 1, biology part). Our recipe is pretty simple-ish: use a bit of partial syntax plus features scooped from a big unlabeled corpas, then treat it like a sequence bio-tagging problem. If we spot a cue, we tag the sentense as uncertain; if not, it’s certain. easy.To try tons of feature mixes, we ran a genetic algorithim that searches combos for us. Some of the features it picks feel kinda weird and hard to explain (tbh, not super interpretable), but they still bumped the system’s perf. In the end, the final system does better than before, even if a few bits are still rough around the edges and could use a lil more tuning. So yeah, the approach mostly works, tho not perfct.

human-2 from Claude AI questions: We present a system for detecting hedge cues—linguistic expressions that signal uncertainty—in biomedical texts as part of the biological track of Task 1 in the CoNLL-2010 shared task. The approach combines partial syntactic information with features derived from an unlabeled corpus and formulates the problem as a sequential BIO-tagging task. When a hedge cue is detected, the corresponding sentence is classified as uncertain; otherwise, it is labeled as certain. To explore a large space of feature combinations, a genetic algorithm is employed. Although some of the features identified through this process are difficult to interpret, they contribute to improved performance in the final system.

human-2 from translated questions: We present a sequential labeling approach to hedge cue detection submitted to the biological portion of Task 1 of the CoNLL-2010 shared task. The proposed method combines partial syntactic information with features obtained from an unlabeled corpus and reformulates hedge cue detection as a sequential BIO-tagging problem. If a hedge cue is detected in a sentence, the sentence is classified as uncertain; otherwise, it is classified as certain. To explore a large number of feature combinations, a genetic algorithm is employed, and although some of the selected features are difficult to interpret, they are shown to improve the performance of the final system.

selected_abstract: we report on results of combining graphical modeling techniques with information extraction resources ( pattern dictionary and lexicon ) for both frame and semantic role assignment . our approach demonstrates the use of two human built knowledge bases ( wordnet and framenet ) for the task of semantic extraction .
human_abstract: We mixed grafical modeling with info-extraction tools (a pattern dictonary + a lexicon) to label both frames and the semantic roles in text. Think of it like: the models handle the structurey bits, while the IE resources give us handy cues from real phrases. We also plugged in two human-built knowledge bases wordnet and framenet to guide the semantic pulling-out step. Result: cleaner frame/role asignments than using any single thing alone. It’s not magic, but the combo plays nice together and makes the extractor a fair bit smarter, even if some edges are still a lil rough.

selected_abstract: in this paper , we will compare and evaluate the effectiveness of different statistical methods in the task of cross-document coreference resolution . we created entity models for different test sets and compare the following disambiguation and clustering techniques to cluster the entity models in order to create coreference chains : incremental vector space kl-divergence agglomerative vector space
human_abstract: We’re comparing how well a few stats methods link mentions of the same entitiy across different docments (aka cross-document coreferece). First we build simple entity models for each name/mention in a couple test sets, then we try to group them into clean coref chains. The methods we test are: (1) an incremental vector-space approach that updates the profile as new articals show up, (2) a KL-divergence based disambig step that compares probability distribtions, and (3) an agglomorative vector-space clustring baseline. Then we check which setup preforms best at making coherent chains some do better than others, but none are perfect.

selected_abstract:
user-driven development of the sonar corpus interfaces
opensonar is an online system that allows for analyzing and searching the large scale dutch reference corpus sonar . due to the size of the corpus , accessing the information contained in the dataset has proven to be difficult for less technically inclined researchers . the opensonar project aims to facilitate the use of the sonar corpus by providing a user-friendly online interface . to make sure that the resulting system is practically useful , several user groups have been identified , who drive the interface development process by providing practical use cases . the current system is already used in educational and research settings .
human_abstract:
We built OpenSonar, an online tool for analysing and searching the big Dutch reference corpus (SoNaR). The corpus is massive, which made it kinda hard for less techy reseachers to get at the good stuff. So the goal here is a friendlier intreface that lets people browse, search, and explore without needing command-line foo.
To make sure it’s actually useful (not just fancy buttons), we lined up a few user groups teachers, students, corpus folks, and other lang-people and asked them for practial use-cases. Their feedback basically drives what we build next: what views matter, which filters are needed, and how results should be shown.
The current system is alreadly in use in eductional settings and reseach projects. It’s not perfect yet, but the user-driven bits keep nudging the UI in the right direction, so more people can work with the SoNaR data without jumping thru tech hoops.

selected_abstract:
the benefit of stochastic pp attachment to a rule-based parser
to study pp attachment disambiguation as a benchmark for empirical methods in natural language processing it has often been reduced to a binary decision problem ( between verb or noun attachment ) in a particular syntactic configuration . a parser , however , must solve the more general task of deciding between more than two alternatives in many different contexts . we combine the attachment predictions made by a simple model of lexical attraction with a full-fledged parser of german to determine the actual benefit of the subtask to parsing . we show that the combination of data-driven and rule-based components can reduce the number of all parsing errors by 14 % and raise the attachment accuracy for dependency parsing of german to an unprecedented 92 % .
human_abstract:
We looked at the old headache of PP attachment you know, deciding whether a prepositional phrase sticks to the verb or the noun. Lots of benchmarks make it a neat little binary thing (verb vs. noun) in one tidy setup, but a real praser has to juggle more than two choices across tons of messy contexts. So we mixed a super simple lexical attraaction model (it guesses where a PP “likes” to go) with a full-on Greman parser to see if this subtask actually helps the whole pipeline, not just the toy case.
Turns out the combo of data-driven bits + rule-based logic plays nice together: we cut overall parsing errors by about 14% and pushed PP-attachment accuracy for German dep parsing up to around 92%, which is kinda unprecedented for this setup. Not gonna lie, some edges are still rough (and a few decisions feel a bit hand-wavy), but the hybrid setup does real work instead of just looking clever on paper. Pretty solid win, even if it’s not perfct.

human-2 from Claude AI questions: In this study, we examine the problem of prepositional phrase (PP) attachment, a well-known source of ambiguity in syntactic parsing. Previous empirical studies often reduce PP attachment to a binary decision between verb and noun attachment within a specific syntactic configuration. In practice, however, a parser must address the more general problem of selecting among multiple attachment alternatives across a variety of contexts. To investigate the practical benefit of this subtask for full parsing, we combine the attachment predictions of a simple lexical attraction model with a rule-based parser for German. The resulting hybrid system integrates data-driven and rule-based components, reducing the total number of parsing errors by 14% and increasing PP attachment accuracy for German dependency parsing to 92%

human-2 from DeepSeek questions: We investigate the benefit of stochastic prepositional phrase (PP) attachment for a rule-based parser. In natural language processing research, PP attachment disambiguation is often simplified as a binary decision problem between verb and noun attachment within a fixed syntactic configuration. However, practical parsers must resolve attachments among more than two alternatives across diverse contexts. To evaluate the contribution of this subtask to full parsing, we combine attachment predictions from a simple lexical attraction model with a rule-based German parser. Our results show that integrating data-driven predictions with rule-based parsing reduces overall parsing errors by 14% and increases PP attachment accuracy in German dependency parsing to 92%. These findings demonstrate that combining statistical and rule-based components can significantly improve parsing performance for complex attachment decisions.

However, a full parser must handle multiple attachment alternatives across diverse syntactic configurations. In this work, we integrate predictions from a lexical attraction model—which estimates attachment preferences based on statistical co-occurrence patterns of words—with a rule-based dependency parser for German to assess the benefit of incorporating PP attachment subtasks into parsing. The combined system reduces the total number of parsing errors by 14% and achieves a PP-attachment accuracy of 92% for German dependency parsing. These results demonstrate that hybrid integration of data-driven statistical cues with rule-based parsing can significantly improve parsing performance over either approach in isolation.

selected_abstract:
a cohesion graph based approach for unsupervised recognition of literal and non-literal use of multiword expressions
we present a graph-based model for representing the lexical cohesion of a discourse . in the graph structure , vertices correspond to the content words of a text and edges connecting pairs of words encode how closely the words are related semantically . we show that such a structure can be used to distinguish literal and non-literal usages of multi-word expressions .
human_abstract: We use a simple graph-y trick to model how words in a text stick together aka lexical coehsion. Each important word becomes a vertex, and we draw edeges between pairs that are sematic-ally close or show up together alot; stronger links mean tighter relatedness. With this unsupervized setup, the graph helps us tell when a multi-word expression is being used literal vs non-literal. So if a phrase like “spill the beans” is surrounded by talk about secrets/gossip, the graph nudges it toward figurative; if it’s near pots, kitchens, and actual beans, it leans literal. It’s pretty lightweight (no fancy labels or hand-coded rules), kinda robust across different texts, and while not perfct, it does a decent job spliting the two uses without much fuss.

selected_abstract:
alternative phrases and natural language information division of informatics
this paper presents a formal analysis for a large class of words called alternative markers , which includes other ( than ) , such ( as ) , and besides . these words appear frequently enough in dialog to warrant serious attention , yet present natural language search engines perform poorly on queries containing them . i show that the performance of a search engine can be improved dramatically by incorporating an approximation of the formal analysis that is compatible with the search engines operational semantics . the value of this approach is that as the operational semantics of natural language applications improve , even larger improvements are possible .
human_abstract: We look at a big family of words I call “alternative markers” things like “other (than)”, “such (as)”, and “besides.” These show up freqently in everyday talk and search queries, but most natural-language search engins kinda stumble when those words are present. In the paper, I give a formal analaysis of how these markers work basically how they divide up info in a sentence and why they confuse search. The trick is: you don’t need the full heavy math to get wins. If you plug a light-weight aproximation of that analysis into a search engine in a way that matches the engine’s operatoinal semantcs, results get way better on those tricky queries. Even nicer, as the application’s semantics improve over time, this same approach scales up—yielding even bigger improvments without tons of extra hackery. Bottom line: handle “other than,” “such as,” and “besides” a bit smarter → fewer dumb misses and more on-point hits. Not perfct, but def a solid step.

selected_abstract:
a markov logic approach to bio-molecular event extraction
in this paper we describe our entry to the bionlp 2009 shared task regarding biomolecular event extraction . our work can be described by three design decisions : ( 1 ) instead of building a pipeline using local classifier technology , we design and learn a joint probabilistic model over events in a sentence ; ( 2 ) instead of developing specific inference and learning algorithms for our joint model , we apply markov logic , a general purpose statistical relation learning language , for this task ; ( 3 ) we represent events as relational structures over the tokens of a sentence , as opposed to structures that explicitly mention abstract event entities . our results are competitive : we achieve the 4th best scores for task 1 ( in close range to the 3rd place ) and the best results for task 2 with a 13 percent point margin .
human_abstract: We joined the BioNLP’09 shared task on bio-molecular event extraction and went with a pretty simple-but-bold idea: instead of a pipeline of local clasifiers, we train one joint probablistic model that looks at all the events in a sentense together. Rather than cooking up special infernce or learining code, we just used Markov Logic a general-purpose statistical relational thing so we could model constraints + probabilities in one place. Also, we represent events as relations over the tokns (who’s connected to who in the sentence) instead of inventing extra “event” objects floating around. How’d it do? Pretty decent tbh: we got 4th on Task 1 (basically neck-and-neck with 3rd), and best on Task 2 with about a 13 percentage-point margin. Not perfct, but the joint + Markov-logic combo clearly helped, and we didn’t need funky custom algos to get there.

selected_abstract:
confidence measure for word alignment
in this paper we present a confidence measure for word alignment based on the posterior probability of alignment links . we introduce sentence alignment confidence measure and alignment link confidence measure . based on these measures , we improve the alignment quality by selecting high confidence sentence alignments and alignment links from multiple word alignments of the same sentence pair . additionally , we remove low confidence alignment links from the word alignment of a bilingual training corpus , which increases the alignment f-score , improves chinese-english and arabic-english translation quality and significantly reduces the phrase translation table size .
human_abstract: We propose a simple confidence score for word alignment that looks at the posterior probablity of each link between words. There are two pieces: a sentence-level confidence (how sure we are the whole sentence pair is aligned well) and a link-level confidence (how sure we are about each word↔word link). Using these, we take multiple alignments for the same sentense pair and keep the high-confidence bits, tossing the sketchy ones. We also prune low-confidence links from the big bilingal training data. Net result: higher alignment F-score, better Chinese↔English and Arabic↔English tranlsation quality, and a way smaller phrase table (less bloat, faster lookups). It’s not perfct and some edges are a lil fuzzy, but picking the solid links and ditching the weak ones gave us a pretty nice improvment overall.

selected_abstract:
an optimal-time binarization algorithm for linear context-free rewriting systems with fan-out two
linear context-free rewriting systems ( lcfrss ) are grammar formalisms with the capability of modeling discontinuous constituents . many applications use lcfrss where the fan-out ( a measure of the discontinuity of phrases ) is not allowed to be greater than 2. we present an efficient algorithm for transforming lcfrs with fan-out at most 2 into a binary form , whenever this is possible . this results in asymptotical run-time improvement for known parsing algorithms for this class .
human_abstract: We look at LCFRS a grammer formalism that can handle discontinous bits in a sentence. In lots of apps people keep the fan-out (how many separate pieces a phrase can split into) to no more than 2. We give an optmal-time algorithim for turning any LCFRS with fan-out ≤ 2 into a binary form (rules combine things two-at-a-time), whenever that’s actually possible.Why bother? Binarizing shrinks the branching, so known parsing methods for this class run faster asymptoticaly. In plain words: same language power, fewer wide rules, better run-time. The approach is pretty efficent, and doesn’t require fancy extra tricks just the right transformations. Bottom line: if your LCFRS sticks to fan-out two, we can binari-ize it in optimal time and your parser gets a speed bump, in theory and usually in pratice too.

human-2 from Claude AI questions: This paper investigates linear context-free rewriting systems (LCFRS), a grammar formalism capable of modeling discontinuous constituents. In many applications, the fan-out, which measures the degree of discontinuity of phrases, is restricted to at most two. We present an optimal-time algorithm for transforming LCFRS with fan-out not exceeding two into an equivalent binary form whenever such a transformation is possible. Binarization restructures grammar rules so that they combine two elements at a time, which leads to asymptotic runtime improvements for known parsing algorithms applied to this class of grammars. The proposed method therefore preserves the expressive power of the grammar while improving the computational efficiency of parsing.

selected_abstract:
hierarchical discriminative classification for text-based geolocation
text-based document geolocation is commonly rooted in language-based information retrieval techniques over geodesic grids . these methods ignore the natural hierarchy of cells in such grids and fall afoul of independence assumptions . we demonstrate the effectiveness of using logistic regression models on a hierarchy of nodes in the grid , which improves upon the state of the art accuracy by several percent and reduces mean error distances by hundreds of kilometers on data from twitter , wikipedia , and flickr . we also show that logistic regression performs feature selection effectively , assigning high weights to geocentric terms .
human_abstract:
We’re trying to guess where a piece of text comes from on the map (text-based geo-location). A lot of older methods chop the world into a geodesic grid and do IR-style matching, but they ignore the natural heirarchy of cells (big areas → smaller ones) and kinda assume cells are independent (they’re not, lol).
Our fix: use a hierarchial, coarse-to-fine stack of logisitc regression models over the grid’s nodes pick a broad region first, then zoom into finer cells. This simple discrimnative setup bumps accurracy by a few percent and cuts mean error distences by hundereds of kilometers on Twitter, Wikipedia, and Flickr. Bonus: logistic regression basically does feature selection for free, pushing high weights on geo-centric words (city names, local lingo, etc). Not magic, but a clean win with a pretty sane model, even if a few edges are still a bit rough.

Many existing approaches rely on language-based information retrieval techniques applied to geodesic grids, but these methods ignore the natural hierarchical structure of grid cells and rely on independence assumptions. To address this limitation, we apply logistic regression models over a hierarchy of nodes within the grid. This hierarchical discriminative approach improves state-of-the-art accuracy by several percent and reduces mean error distances by hundreds of kilometers on datasets from Twitter, Wikipedia, and Flickr. selected_abstract:
a system to solve language tests for second grade students
this paper describes a system which solves language tests for second grade students ( 7 years old ) . in japan , there are materials for students to measure understanding of what they studied , just like sat for high school students in us . we use textbooks for the students as the target material of this study . questions in the materials are classified into four types : questions about chinese character ( kanji ) , about word knowledge , reading comprehension , and composition . this program doesnt resolve the composition and some other questions which are not easy to be implemented in text forms . we built a subsystem for each finer type of questions . as a result , we achieved 55 % - 83 % accuracy in answering questions in unseen materials .
human_abstract: We built a simple system to help solve langauge tests for 2nd graders (about 7 y/o). In Japan kids get practice booklets to check what they learned kinda like tiny SATs for US highschoolers. We point our program at the same textboks they study from. The questions fall into four buckets: Kanji (Chinese charcters), word knowlege, reading comprehension, and composition. Our program doesnt really handle composition (and a few other super free-form ones), so we skip those for now. For the rest we made a small subsytem for each type so it can focus better. On totally unseeen materials it gets about 55–83% accuarcy, depending on the section so not perfct, but pretty decent for a first go, and we can prob polish it more.

selected_abstract:
classifying japanese polysemous verbs based on fuzzy c-means
this paper presents a method for classifying japanese polysemous verbs using an algorithm to identify overlapping nodes with more than one cluster . the algorithm is a graph-based unsupervised clustering algorithm , which combines a generalized modularity function , spectral mapping , and fuzzy clustering technique . the modularity function for measuring cluster structure is calculated based on the frequency distributions over verb frames with selectional preferences . evaluations are made on two sets of verbs including polysemies .
human_abstract: We’re grouping Japanese verbs with many meanings (polysemous verbs), but we don’t force a verb into just one bucket some meanings overlap, so a word can live in more than one clustr. Our algorithim is graph-based and unsupervized: we build a graph from how verbs show up in verb frames (incl. their selectional preferneces), then use a mix of spectral mapping  a generalized modulairty score to see where the structure is, and finally fuzzy c-means  so memberships can be soft instead of all-or-nothing. The modulairty is computed from the frequncy patterns over those frames, which tells us how tightly verbs hang together. We ran evals on two verb sets (with lots of polyesmies) and the method sorts them pretty well showing overlapping senses are real and the fuzzy approach catches them, even if a few edges are still a lil messy.

The modularity function for measuring cluster structure is computed from the frequency distributions over verb frames with selectional preferences. Spectral mapping is used together with the modularity function to represent the graph structure of verb relations, and fuzzy clustering enables verbs to belong to multiple clusters, which is suitable for representing polysemy. The proposed method is evaluated on two sets of verbs containing polysemous cases, demonstrating its ability to identify overlapping cluster structures for Japanese verbs.

selected_abstract:
challenges for extracting biomedical knowledge from full text
at present , most biomedical information retrieval and extraction tools process abstracts rather than full-text articles . the increasing availability of full text will allow more knowledge to be extracted with greater reliability . to investigate the challenges of full-text processing , we manually annotated a corpus of cited articles from a molecular interaction map ( kohn , 1999 ) . our analysis demonstrates the necessity of full-text processing ; identifies the article sections where interactions are most commonly stated ; and quantifies both the amount of external knowledge required and the proportion of interactions requiring multiple or deeper inference steps . further , it identifies a range of nlp tools required , including : identifying synonyms , and resolving coreference and negated expressions . this is important guidance for researchers engineering biomedical text processing systems .
human_abstract: Right now most bio-medical text tools read abstracts, not the whole paper. As more full-text becomes availablity, we can pull out more facts with better reliablity. To see what’s tricky, we hand-annotated a bunch of papers cited by a molecular interaction map (Kohn, 1999) and analysed what’s really going on. Turns out full text is kinda necessary: we identifed which secitons most often state the actual interactions, how much outside/backgroud knowledge you need, and how often you must do multi-step or deeper inferencing (not just one hop).Also, you need a decent NLP toolbox: spot synonmys, resolve co-reference (who “it/they” refers to), and handle negated lines so you don’t record things that aren’t true. Bottom line: if you’re building biomed text processing, don’t stop at abstracts go full text, or you’ll miss alot, tbh.

human-2 from Claude AI questions: Most biomedical information retrieval and extraction systems currently process abstracts rather than full-text articles. However, the growing availability of full-text publications provides opportunities to extract knowledge with greater reliability. To investigate the challenges associated with full-text processing, we manually annotated a corpus of articles cited in a molecular interaction map (Kohn, 1999). Our analysis demonstrates the necessity of processing full-text documents and identifies the sections of articles in which molecular interactions are most frequently reported. In addition, the study quantifies both the extent of external knowledge required for accurate extraction and the proportion of interactions that require multiple or deeper inference steps beyond single-step extraction. The analysis further highlights the importance of several natural language processing capabilities for reliable biomedical information extraction, including synonym identification, coreference resolution, and the handling of negated expressions. These findings provide practical guidance for the design and development of biomedical text processing systems capable of effectively extracting knowledge from full-text scientific articles.

selected_abstract:
a flexemic tagset for polish
the article notes certain weaknesses of current efforts aiming at the standardization of pos tagsets for morphologically rich languages and argues that , in order to achieve clear mappings between tagsets , it is necessary to have clear and formal rules of delimiting poss and grammatical categories within any given tagset . an attempt at constructing such a tagset for polish is presented .
human_abstract: We point out some problems with the current standarization of POS tagsets for morphologically rich langauges. A lot of tags kinda mush together part-of-speech and grammer features (case, gender, etc.), so mapping one tagset to another becomes messy. Our take: if you want clean mappings, you need clear, formal rules that say where POS ends and where grammatical cateogries begin otherwise it’s apples vs. oranges. So we sketch a flexemic tagset for Polsih that tries to draw those lines crisply. The idea is to keep POS labels lean and push inflectional stuff into well-defined feature slots, with rules that are explicit (not hand-wavy). With that, cross-walking between tagsets gets a lot less painful, even if a few edges still feel a bit fuzzy and will need more polish (no pun intennded).

selected_abstract:
support vector machines for paraphrase identification and corpus construction
the lack of readily-available large corpora of aligned monolingual sentence pairs is a major obstacle to the development of statistical machine translation-based paraphrase models . in this paper , we describe the use of annotated datasets and support vector machines to induce larger monolingual paraphrase corpora from a comparable corpus of news clusters found on the world wide web . features include : morphological variants ; wordnet synonyms and hypernyms ; loglikelihood-based word pairings dynamically obtained from baseline sentence alignments ; and formal string features such as word-based edit distance . use of this technique dramatically reduces the alignment error rate of the extracted corpora over heuristic methods based on position of the sentences in the text .
human_abstract: The topic of this study is SVM-based paraphrase identification. The issue is that there isn't a large corpus for this, making training challenging. They are building to solve it using a few annotated datasets after creating a corpus from online news data. They make use of morphology, but it's unclear, and features like edit distance and synonyms. The system performs better than merely employing techniques, such as merely verifying the position of sentences.

human-2 from Claude AI questions: This paper addresses the scarcity of large corpora of aligned monolingual sentence pairs, which limits the development of statistical machine-translation–based paraphrase models. The authors propose a method for constructing larger paraphrase corpora by applying support vector machines (SVMs) to annotated datasets derived from comparable corpora of news clusters collected from the web. The SVM classifier incorporates multiple feature types, including morphological variants, lexical relations from WordNet such as synonyms and hypernyms, log-likelihood–based word pairings obtained from baseline sentence alignments, and string similarity features such as word-based edit distance. These features enable the system to identify paraphrase relationships more accurately within clusters of related news articles. Experimental results show that the proposed approach substantially reduces the alignment error rate in the extracted paraphrase corpus compared with heuristic methods that rely primarily on sentence position within documents.

selected_abstract:
dale : a word sense disambiguation system for biomedical documents
automatic interpretation of documents is hampered by the fact that language contains terms which have multiple meanings . these ambiguities can still be found when language is restricted to a particular domain , such as biomedicine . word sense disambiguation ( wsd ) systems attempt to resolve these ambiguities but are often only able to identify the meanings for a small set of ambiguous terms . dale ( disambiguation using automatically labeled examples ) is a supervised wsd system that can disambiguate a wide range of ambiguities found in biomedical documents . dale uses the umls metathesaurus as both a sense inventory and as a source of information for automatically generating labeled training examples . dale is able to disambiguate biomedical documents with the coverage of unsupervised approaches and accuracy of supervised methods .
human_abstract: The DALE system for word sense disambiguation in biomedical texts is the subject of this research. Many words in papers can have many meanings, and the biomedical field is no exception. DALE can handle more terms than the typical WSD systems. It is supervised and automatically generates training examples and the sense list using the UMLS metathesaurus. The system's coverage is similar to that of unsupervised systems, but its accuracy is closer to that of supervised ones.

human-2 from Claude AI questions: This paper presents DALE (Disambiguation using Automatically Labeled Examples), a supervised word sense disambiguation system designed for biomedical documents. Ambiguity remains a significant challenge in automatic document interpretation because many terms can express multiple meanings, even within specialized domains such as biomedicine. While existing WSD systems often focus on a limited set of ambiguous terms, DALE aims to address a broader range of ambiguities. The system employs the UMLS Metathesaurus both as a sense inventory and as a resource for automatically generating labeled training examples, enabling supervised learning without extensive manual annotation. As a result, DALE achieves the broad coverage typically associated with unsupervised approaches while maintaining the higher accuracy commonly obtained by supervised methods in biomedical word sense disambiguation.

selected_abstract: we present a sequential labeling approach to hedge cue detection submitted to the biological portion of task 1 for the conll2010 shared task . our main approach is as follows . we make use of partial syntactic information together with features obtained from the unlabeled corpus , and convert the task into one of sequential biotagging . if a cue is found , a sentence is classified as uncertain and certain otherwise . to examine a large number of feature combinations , we employ a genetic algorithm . while some features obtained by this method are difficult to interpret , they were shown to improve the performance of the final system .
human_abstract: This paper outlines an automatic way of discovering "hedge cues" (phrases or words that convey uncertainty) in text, particularly biological text, as part of a competition (CoNLL 2010 shared task). Utilizing some syntactic data and unlabelled text features. Approaching the issue as a sequence labeling task (BIOTagging), where every word is labeled for whether it belongs to a hedge cue.If a cue is found, the sentence is labeled as "uncertain"; otherwise, it's "certain." They employed a genetic algorithm to search through numerous combinations of features, even though some of these features were difficult to interpret.

selected_abstract:
evaluation technology from speaker identification to affective analysis : a multi-step system for analyzing childrens stories
we propose a multi-step system for the analysis of childrens stories that is intended to be part of a larger text-to-speechbased storytelling system . a hybrid approach is adopted , where pattern-based and statistical methods are used along with utilization of external knowledge sources . this system performs the following story analysis tasks : identification of characters in each story ; attribution of quotes to specific story characters ; identification of character age , gender and other salient personality attributes ; and finally , affective analysis of the quoted material . the different types of analyses were evaluated using several datasets . for the quote attribution , as well as for the gender and age estimation , substantial improvement over baseline was realized , whereas results for personality attribute estimation and valence estimation are more modest .
human_abstract: This research work outlines ​​a multi-step system for analyzing children's stories.  The long-term goal is to deploy this system within a larger text-to-speech storytelling platform. The system uses a hybrid approach, combining pattern-based and statistical methods, and also includes external knowledge to perform several key analysis tasks:  It reports the various characters appearing in each story;  It attributes spoken lines (quotes) to the particular character who uttered them; It specifies a character's age, gender, and other salient personality features; Lastly, it examines the affect (emotional content) of the quoted text. The authors tested each of these analysis types on a range of datasets. They found significant improvements over baseline approaches for quote attribution, gender, and age estimation. The end goal for personality attribute estimation and valence (positive/negative emotional content) were more modest.

selected_abstract:

human_abstract: The usefulness of stochastic PP (prepositional phrase) attachment for a rule-based parser is examined in this paper. The authors combine a thorough rule-based parser for German with attachment predictions from a basic lexical attraction model. Their results show that parsing performance is greatly enhanced by this combination of rule-based and data-driven components. In particular, it improves the attachment accuracy for German dependency parsing to a remarkable 92% and lowers the overall number of parsing errors by 14%.

a markov logic approach to bio-molecular event extraction
in this paper we describe our entry to the bionlp 2009 shared task regarding biomolecular event extraction . our work can be described by three design decisions : ( 1 ) instead of building a pipeline using local classifier technology , we design and learn a joint probabilistic model over events in a sentence ; ( 2 ) instead of developing specific inference and learning algorithms for our joint model , we apply markov logic , a general purpose statistical relation learning language , for this task ; ( 3 ) we represent events as relational structures over the tokens of a sentence , as opposed to structures that explicitly mention abstract event entities . our results are competitive : we achieve the 4th best scores for task 1 ( in close range to the 3rd place ) and the best results for task 2 with a 13 percent point margin .

selected_abstract:
support vector machines for paraphrase identification and corpus construction
the lack of readily-available large corpora of aligned monolingual sentence pairs is a major obstacle to the development of statistical machine translation-based paraphrase models . in this paper , we describe the use of annotated datasets and support vector machines to induce larger monolingual paraphrase corpora from a comparable corpus of news clusters found on the world wide web . features include : morphological variants ; wordnet synonyms and hypernyms ; loglikelihood-based word pairings dynamically obtained from baseline sentence alignments ; and formal string features such as word-based edit distance . use of this technique dramatically reduces the alignment error rate of the extracted corpora over heuristic methods based on position of the sentences in the text .
human_abstract: A major obstacle in creating statistical machine translation (SMT)-based paraphrase models is the lack of large, aligned monolingual sentence corpora, which is addressed in this paper. The authors suggest a technique for inducing larger paraphrase corpora that makes use of Support Vector Machines (SVMs) and already-existing annotated datasets. This is accomplished by selecting potential paraphrases from similar news clusters on the Internet. Morphological variants, WordNet synonyms and hypernyms, log-likelihood-based word pairings derived from baseline sentence alignments, and formal string features like word-based edit distance are just a few of the features that the SVMs use. When compared to heuristic approaches based on sentence position, this method is demonstrated to significantly lower the alignment error rate of the extracted corpora.

selected_abstract:
the web as a baseline : evaluating the performance of unsupervised web-based models for a range of nlp tasks
previous work demonstrated that web counts can be used to approximate bigram frequencies , and thus should be useful for a wide variety of nlp tasks . so far , only two generation tasks ( candidate selection for machine translation and confusion-set disambiguation ) have been tested using web-scale data sets . the present paper investigates if these results generalize to tasks covering both syntax and semantics , both generation and analysis , and a larger range of n-grams . for the majority of tasks , we find that simple , unsupervised models perform better when n-gram frequencies are obtained from the web rather than from a large corpus . however , in most cases , web-based models fail to outperform more sophisticated state-of-theart models trained on small corpora . we argue that web-based models should therefore be used as a baseline for , rather than an alternative to , standard models .
human_abstract: The broad applicability of using web-derived n-gram frequencies for different NLP tasks is assessed in this paper. Although earlier studies showed that web counts could be useful for bigram frequencies in a few generation tasks, this study explores whether these advantages hold true for tasks that cover syntax and semantics, both generation and analysis, and a larger variety of n-grams. The results show that using n-gram frequencies from the web rather than a large corpus does indeed improve the performance of simple, unsupervised models. These web-based models, however, typically fall short of more advanced, cutting-edge models that have been trained on more specialised, smaller corpora. As a result, the authors contend that rather than serving as a direct substitute, web-based models ought to serve as a foundation for conventional NLP models.

selected_abstract:
dale : a word sense disambiguation system for biomedical documents
automatic interpretation of documents is hampered by the fact that language contains terms which have multiple meanings . these ambiguities can still be found when language is restricted to a particular domain , such as biomedicine . word sense disambiguation ( wsd ) systems attempt to resolve these ambiguities but are often only able to identify the meanings for a small set of ambiguous terms . dale ( disambiguation using automatically labeled examples ) is a supervised wsd system that can disambiguate a wide range of ambiguities found in biomedical documents . dale uses the umls metathesaurus as both a sense inventory and as a source of information for automatically generating labeled training examples . dale is able to disambiguate biomedical documents with the coverage of unsupervised approaches and accuracy of supervised methods .
human_abstract: In this paper, a supervised Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) system for biomedical documents called DALE (Disambiguation Using Automatically Labelled Examples) is presented. The main issue addressed is that automatic document interpretation is hampered by the inherent ambiguity of language, even in specialised fields. DALE seeks to disambiguate a broad range of ambiguities present in biomedical text, whereas conventional WSD systems frequently only handle a small number of ambiguous terms. It uses the UMLS Metathesaurus as a source of potential meanings (sense inventory) and as a tool to produce labelled training examples automatically. As a result, DALE combines the accuracy of supervised techniques with the wide coverage usually found in unsupervised approaches.

selected_abstract:
combining prediction by partial matching and logistic regression for thai word segmentation
word segmentation is an important part of many applications , including information retrieval , information filtering , document analysis , and text summarization . in thai language , the process is complicated since words are written continuously , and their structures are not well-defined . a recognized effective approach to word segmentation is longest matching , a method based on dictionary . nevertheless , this method suffers from character-level and syllable-level ambiguities in determining word boundaries . this paper proposes a technique to thai word segmentation using a two-step approach . first , text is segmented , using an application of prediction by partial matching , into syllables whose structures are more well-defined . this reduces the earlier type of ambiguity . then , the syllables are combined into words by an application of a syllable-level longest matching method together with a logistic regression model which takes into account contextual information . the experimental results show the syllable segmentation accuracy of more than 96.65 % and the overall word segmentation accuracy of 97 % .
human_abstract: The intricacies of Thai word segmentation a critical task for numerous NLP applications are discussed in this paper. This task is hampered by continuous writing and vague word structures. Despite being widely used, dictionary-based longest matching has trouble with ambiguities at the character and syllable levels. The authors suggest a two-step method. In order to reduce initial ambiguities, it first uses Prediction by Partial Matching (PPM) to segment text into more distinct syllables. Second, a logistic regression model that takes contextual information into account is combined with a syllable-level longest matching technique to combine these syllables into words. The experimental results show high accuracy, with an overall word segmentation accuracy of 97% and a syllable segmentation accuracy of over 96.65%.

we study the problem of integrating scattered online opinions . for this purpose , we propose to exploit structured ontology to obtain well-formed relevant aspects to a topic and use them to organize scattered opinions to generate a structured summary . particularly , we focus on two main challenges in implementing this idea , ( 1 ) how to select the most useful aspects from a large number of aspects in the ontology and ( 2 ) how to order the selected aspects to optimize the readability of the structured summary . we propose and explore several methods for solving these challenges . experimental results on two different data sets ( us presidents and digital cameras ) show that the proposed methods are effective for selecting aspects that can represent the major opinions and for generating coherent ordering of aspects .

Results from experiments on datasets related to "US Presidents" and "Digital Cameras" show how well their techniques work at choosing elements that reflect key viewpoints and creating a logical hierarchy for them.

selected_abstract:
parsing syntactic and semantic dependencies with two single-stage maximum entropy models
this paper describes our system to carry out the joint parsing of syntactic and semantic dependencies for our participation in the shared task of conll-2008 . we illustrate that both syntactic parsing and semantic parsing can be transformed into a word-pair classification problem and implemented as a single-stage system with the aid of maximum entropy modeling . our system ranks the fourth in the closed track for the task with the following performance on the wsj+brown test set : 81.44 % labeled macro f1 for the overall task , 86.66 % labeled attachment for syntactic dependencies , and 76.16 % labeled f1 for semantic dependencies .
human_abstract: The system designed for the CoNLL-2008 shared task, which focusses on the joint parsing of syntactic and semantic dependencies, is described in this paper. The main idea is to reframe syntactic and semantic parsing as a word-pair classification problem. A single-stage system that uses Maximum Entropy modelling then tackles this problem. The system placed fourth in the closed track, achieving competitive results. It showed an 81.44% labelled macro F1-score for the overall task, an 86.66% labelled attachment accuracy for syntactic dependencies, and a 76.16% labelled F1-score for semantic dependencies on the WSJ+Brown test set.

selected_abstract:
towards the automatic creation of a wordnet from a term-based lexical
the work described here aims to create a wordnet automatically from a semantic network based on terms . so , a clustering procedure is ran over a synonymy network , in order to obtain synsets . then , the term arguments of each relational triple are assigned to the latter , originating a wordnet . experiments towards our goal are reported and their results validated .
human_abstract: The project described in this paper aims to automatically build a WordNet from an existing term-based semantic network. In order to find synsets (sets of synonymous words), a synonymy network is first subjected to a clustering procedure. Second, the WordNet structure is created by assigning the term arguments found in each relational triple to these newly created synsets. In order to achieve this, experiments were carried out, and the results were validated.

selected_abstract: mimus : a multimodal and multilingual dialogue system for the home this paper describes mimus , a multimodal and multilingual dialogue system for the in home scenario , which allows users to control some home devices by voice and/or clicks . its design relies on wizard of oz experiments and is targeted at disabled users . mimus follows the information state update approach to dialogue management , and supports english , german and spanish , with the possibility of changing language onthe fly . mimus includes a gesturesenabled talking head which endows the system with a humanlike personality .
human_abstract: MIMUS, a multimodal and multilingual dialogue system intended for domestic settings, is described in this paper. MIMUS allows users to click and speak to control their home devices. It was created with disabled users in mind, drawing inspiration from Wizard of Oz experiments. English, German, and Spanish are supported by MIMUS, which uses an information state update method for managing dialogues and allows for real-time language switching. The system is notable for having a talking head that can be activated by gestures, which gives it a human-like personality.

selected_abstract:
plans toward automated chat summarization
we describe the beginning stages of our work on summarizing chat , which is motivated by our observations concerning the information overload of us navy watchstanders . we describe the challenges of summarizing chat and focus on two chat-specific types of summarizations we are interested in : thread summaries and temporal summaries . we then discuss our plans for addressing these challenges and evaluation issues .

selected_abstract:
serial combination of rules and statistics : a case study in czech
a hybrid system is described which combines the strength of manual rulewriting and statistical learning , obtaining results superior to both methods if applied separately . the combination of a rule-based system and a statistical one is not parallel but serial : the rule-based system performing partial disambiguation with recall close to 100 % is applied first , and a trigram hmm tagger runs on its results . an experiment in czech tagging has been performed with encouraging results .
human_abstract: In order to achieve better results than either approach alone, this paper presents a hybrid system for Czech tagging that serially combines rule-based and statistical methods. Initially, a rule-based system achieves nearly 100% recall in partial disambiguation. The output of the rule-based system is then subjected to a trigram HMM tagger. Czech tagging experiments showed promising results.

selected_abstract:
accurate word segmentation and pos tagging for japanese microblogs : corpus annotation and joint modeling with lexical normalization
microblogs have recently received widespread interest from nlp researchers . however , current tools for japanese word segmentation and pos tagging still perform poorly on microblog texts . we developed an annotated corpus and proposed a joint model for overcoming this situation . our annotated corpus of microblog texts enables not only training of accurate statistical models but also quantitative evaluation of their performance . our joint model with lexical normalization handles the orthographic diversity of microblog texts . we conducted an experiment to demonstrate that the corpus and model substantially contribute to boosting accuracy .
human_abstract: This work tackles the problem of precise Japanese word segmentation and Part-of-Speech (POS) tagging for microblog posts, an area where existing natural language processing (NLP) tools fall short. In order to get around this, the authors created a specific annotated corpus that makes it easier to train and assess statistical models quantitatively. Tests show that this new corpus and model greatly improves the precision of POS tagging and word segmentation in this difficult field.

selected_abstract:
extracting opinions , opinion holders , and topics expressed in online news media text
this paper presents a method for identifying an opinion with its holder and topic , given a sentence from online news media texts . we introduce an approach of exploiting the semantic structure of a sentence , anchored to an opinion bearing verb or adjective . this method uses semantic role labeling as an intermediate step to label an opinion holder and topic using data from framenet . we decompose our task into three phases : identifying an opinion-bearing word , labeling semantic roles related to the word in the sentence , and then finding the holder and the topic of the opinion word among the labeled semantic roles . for a broader coverage , we also employ a clustering technique to predict the most probable frame for a word which is not defined in framenet . our experimental results show that our system performs significantly better than the baseline .
human_abstract: In this paper, we propose a technique to extract opinions, their holders, and their topics from online news media sentences. The method makes use of a sentence's semantic structure, particularly when it comes to verbs or adjectives that express opinion. Using information from FrameNet, the technique employs Semantic Role Labelling (SRL) as a transitional step to determine the topic and opinion holder. There are three phases to the task: recognising the word that expresses an opinion; identifying the word's semantic functions in the sentence; identifying the topic and the person holding the opinion among these designated semantic roles. The system also uses a clustering technique to predict the most likely semantic frame for words that aren't explicitly defined in FrameNet in order to guarantee wider coverage. Results from experiments show that this system performs noticeably better than baseline techniques.

selected_abstract:
an account for compound prepositions
there are some sorts of preposition + noun combinations in farsi that apparently a prepositional phrase almost behaves as compound prepositions . as they are not completely behaving as compounds , it is doubtful that the process of word formation is a morphological one . the analysis put forward by this paper proposes incorporation by which an no is incorporated to a po constructing a compound preposition . in this way tagging prepositions and parsing texts in natural language processing is defined in a proper manner .
human_abstract: This study looks at particular preposition + noun combinations in Farsi that behave like they aren't fully formed morphological compounds but functionally resemble compound prepositions. The authors contend that this method offers a more suitable framework for Farsi text parsing and preposition tagging for applications involving natural language processing.

selected_abstract:
constructing a practical constituent parser from a japanese treebank
we present an empirical study on constructing a japanese constituent parser , which can output function labels to deal with more detailed syntactic information . japanese syntactic parse trees are usually represented as unlabeled dependency structure between bunsetsu chunks , however , such expression is insufficient to uncover the syntactic information about distinction between complements and adjuncts and coordination structure , which is required for practical applications such as syntactic reordering of machine translation . we describe a preliminary effort on constructing a japanese constituent parser by a penn treebank style treebank semi-automatically made from a dependency-based corpus . the evaluations show the parser trained on the treebank has comparable bracketing accuracy as conventional bunsetsu-based parsers , and can output such function labels as the grammatical role of the argument and the type of adnominal phrases .
human_abstract: An empirical study aimed at developing a useful Japanese constituent parser that can produce function labels for more detailed syntactic information is described in this paper. This study attempts to provide information essential for applications such as machine translation reordering, in contrast to typical Japanese dependency structures between bunsetsu chunks, which lack specific syntactic distinctions (e.g., complements vs. adjuncts, coordination). By semi-automatically generating a Penn Treebank-style treebank from an existing dependency-based corpus, the authors describe an initial attempt to build such a parser. Tests demonstrate that the resulting parser, trained on this new treebank, successfully outputs detailed function labels, including grammatical roles of arguments and types of adnominal phrases, while achieving bracketing accuracy comparable to traditional bunsetsu-based parsers.

selected_abstract:
acoustic transformations to improve the intelligibility of dysarthric speech
this paper describes modifications to acoustic speech signals produced by speakers with dysarthria in order to make those utterances more intelligible to typical listeners . these modifications include the correction of tempo , the adjustment of formant frequencies in sonorants , the removal of aberrant voicing , the deletion of phoneme insertion errors , and the replacement of erroneously dropped phonemes . through simple evaluations of intelligibility with nave listeners , we show that the correction of phoneme errors results in the greatest increase in intelligibility and is therefore a desirable mechanism for the eventual creation of augmentative application software for individuals with dysarthria .
human_abstract: The acoustic adjustments made to dysarthric speech signals to improve their intelligibility for normal listeners are described in this paper. Tempo correction, sonorant formant frequency adjustments, aberrant voicing removal, insertion phoneme deletion, and dropped phoneme replacement are among the changes. The study shows that phoneme error correction produces the greatest increase in intelligibility through straightforward intelligibility tests with unsuspecting listeners. For the future development of augmentative communication software for people with dysarthria, this finding identifies a critical mechanism.

selected_abstract:
using machine learning techniques to build a comma checker
in this paper , we describe the research using machine learning techniques to build a comma checker to be integrated in a grammar checker for basque . after several experiments , and trained with a little corpus of 100,000 words , the sys tem guesses correctly not placing com mas with a precision of 96 % and a re call of 98 % . it also gets a precision of 70 % and a recall of 49 % in the task of placing commas . finally , we have shown that these results can be improved using a bigger and a more homogeneous corpus to train , that is , a bigger corpus written by one unique author .
human_abstract: The research on creating a machine learning-based Basque comma checker that can be integrated into a more comprehensive grammar checker is described in this paper. Trained on a relatively small 100,000-word corpus, the system achieves strong performance in correctly omitting commas, with 96% precision and 98% recall. It performs more modestly when it comes to correctly placing commas, with 49% recall and 70% precision. The authors conclude that these results can be further improved by utilizing a larger and more homogeneous training corpus, such as one written by a single author.

selected_abstract:
cluster stopping rules for word sense discrimination
as text data becomes plentiful , unsupervised methods for word sense disambiguation ( wsd ) become more viable . a problem encountered in applying wsd methods is finding the exact number of senses an ambiguity has in a training corpus collected in an automated manner . that number is not known a priori ; rather it needs to be determined based on the data itself . we address that problem using cluster stopping methods . such techniques have not previously applied to wsd . we implement the methods of calinski and harabasz ( 1975 ) and hartigan ( 1975 ) and our adaptation of the gap statistic ( tibshirani , walter and hastie , 2001 ) . for evaluation , we use the wsd test set from the national library of medicine , whose sense inventory is the unified medical language system . the best accuracy for selecting the correct number of clusters is 0.60 with the c & h method . our error analysis shows that the cluster stopping methods make finergrained sense distinctions by creating additional clusters . the highest f-scores ( 82.89 ) , indicative of the quality of cluster membership assignment , are comparable to the baseline majority sense ( 82.63 ) and point to a path towards accuracy improvement via additional cluster pruning . the importance and significance of the current work is in applying cluster stopping rules to wsd .
human_abstract: The automatic determination of the correct number of senses for an ambiguous word in a given corpus is a major challenge in unsupervised Word Sense Disambiguation (WSD) that is addressed in this paper. In order to address this, the authors employ a novel technique that has not been employed in WSD before: cluster stopping methods. They apply techniques by Hartigan, Calinski and Harabasz (C&H), and a modified version of the gap statistic. The C&H approach selected the right number of clusters with the best accuracy of 60% when tested on the National Library of Medicine's WSD test set (using UMLS as the sense inventory). Error analysis showed that by producing more clusters, these techniques typically produce finer-grained sense distinctions. Additional cluster pruning may be necessary to improve accuracy further, as the highest F-scores for cluster membership assignment (82.89%) were similar to the majority sense baseline (82.63%). The main contribution is the investigation and innovative use of cluster stopping rules for WSD.

selected_abstract:
effective morphological feature selection
the inclusion of morphological features provides very useful information that helps to enhance the results when parsing morphologically rich languages . maltoptimizer is a tool , that given a data set , searches for the optimal parameters , parsing algorithm and optimal feature set achieving the best results that it can find for parsers trained with maltparser . in this paper , we present an extension of maltoptimizer that explores , one by one and in combination , the features that are geared towards morphology . from our experiments in the context of the shared task on parsing morphologically rich languages , we extract an in-depth study that shows which features are actually useful for transition-based parsing and we provide competitive results , in a fast and simple way .
human_abstract: The goal of this paper is to efficiently choose morphological features in order to improve parsing for morphologically rich languages. It presents an addition to MaltOptimizer, a program that usually determines the best parameters and algorithms for MaltParser. Individual and combined morphology-specific features are systematically explored by the new extension. The authors provide a thorough analysis of which morphological features are actually useful for transition-based parsing through experiments carried out within the shared task on parsing morphologically rich languages. Their results result in simple and effective competitive parsing results.

selected_abstract:
learning image embeddings using convolutional neural networks for improved multi-modal semantics
we construct multi-modal concept representations by concatenating a skip-gram linguistic representation vector with a visual concept representation vector computed using the feature extraction layers of a deep convolutional neural network ( cnn ) trained on a large labeled object recognition dataset . this transfer learning approach brings a clear performance gain over features based on the traditional bag-of-visual-word approach . experimental results are reported on the wordsim353 and men semantic relatedness evaluation tasks . we use visual features computed using either imagenet or esp game images .
human_abstract: In this paper, a technique for integrating visual and linguistic information to create multi-modal concept representations is presented. A visual concept vector taken from the feature layers of a deep Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) that has been pre-trained on a sizable object recognition dataset is concatenated with a skip-gram linguistic vector in this method. It is shown that this transfer learning method performs noticeably better than conventional bag-of-visual-words features. Experimental results on the WordSim353 and MEN semantic relatedness evaluation tasks, which use visual features derived from either ImageNet or ESP Game images, validate the efficacy.

selected_abstract:
semantic dependency parsing using n-best semantic role sequences and roleset information
in this paper , we describe a syntactic and semantic dependency parsing system submitted to the shared task of conll 2008. the proposed system consists of five modules : syntactic dependency parser , predicate identifier , local semantic role labeler , global role sequence candidate generator , and role sequence selector . the syntactic dependency parser is based on malt parser and the sequence candidate generator is based on cky style algorithm . the remaining three modules are implemented by using maximum entropy classifiers . the proposed system achieves 76.90 of labeled f1 for the overall task , 84.82 of labeled attachment , and 68.71 of labeled f1 on the wsj+brown test set .
human_abstract: This paper presents a system for syntactic and semantic dependency parsing that was submitted to the CoNLL 2008 shared task. There are five modules in the suggested system: A parser for syntactic dependencies (based on MaltParser); a predicate identification; A semantic role labeller that is local; CKY-style algorithm-based global role sequence candidate generator; a selector for role sequence. Maximum Entropy classifiers are used to implement the last three modules. The system's labelled F1-score on the WSJ+Brown test set was 76.90 for the overall task, 84.82 for the syntactic labelled attachment, and 68.71 for the semantic labelled F1.

selected_abstract:
integrating cross-lingually relevant news articles and monolingual web documents in bilingual lexicon acquisition
in the framework of bilingual lexicon acquisition from cross-lingually relevant news articles on the web , it is relatively harder to reliably estimate bilingual term correspondences for low frequency terms . considering such a situation , this paper proposes to complementarily use much larger monolingual web documents collected by search engines , as a resource for reliably re-estimating bilingual term correspondences . we experimentally show that , using a sufficient number of monolingual web documents , it is quite possible to have reliable estimate of bilingual term correspondences for those low frequency terms .
human_abstract: The problem of accurately estimating bilingual term correspondences for low-frequency terms during the process of learning bilingual lexicon from cross-lingual news articles is addressed in this paper. In order to get around this, the authors suggest a different strategy: re-estimating these correspondences more accurately by using much larger monolingual web documents that are found through search engines. Results from experiments show that a sufficient number of these monolingual web documents can be used to reliably estimate bilingual term correspondences for these low-frequency terms that were previously problematic.

selected_abstract: combining manual rules and supervised learning for hedge cue and　scope detection hedge cues were detected using a supervised conditional random field ( crf ) classifier exploiting features from the rasp parser . the crfs predictions were filtered using known cues and unseen instances were removed , increasing precision while retaining recall . rules for scope detection , based on the grammatical relations of the sentence and the part-ofspeech tag of the cue , were manuallydeveloped . however , another supervised crf classifier was used to refine these predictions . as a final step , scopes were constructed from the classifier output using a small set of post-processing rules . development of the system revealed a number of issues with the annotation scheme adopted by the organisers .
human_abstract: In this paper, a hybrid system that combines supervised learning and manual rules for hedge cue and scope detection is described. A supervised Conditional Random Field (CRF) classifier that makes use of RASP parser features is first used to identify hedge cues. To increase precision while preserving recall, these CRF predictions are subsequently filtered using recognised cues, and unseen instances are eliminated. Manually created rules based on grammatical relations and the cue's Part-of-Speech tag are used for scope detection. A second supervised CRF classifier further refines these predictions. Lastly, a limited number of post-processing rules are used to build scopes from the classifier's output. The system's development brought to light a number of problems with the organisers' annotation scheme, according to the authors.

selected_abstract: latent mixture of discriminative experts for multimodal prediction modeling during face-to-face conversation , people naturally integrate speech , gestures and higher level language interpretations to predict the right time to start talking or to give backchannel feedback . in this paper we introduce a new model called latent mixture of discriminative experts which addresses some of the key issues with multimodal language processing : ( 1 ) temporal synchrony/asynchrony between modalities , ( 2 ) micro dynamics and ( 3 ) integration of different levels of interpretation . we present an empirical evaluation on listener nonverbal feedback prediction ( e.g. , head nod ) , based on observable behaviors of the speaker . we confirm the importance of combining four types of multimodal features : lexical , syntactic structure , eye gaze , and prosody . we show that our latent mixture of discriminative experts model outperforms previous approaches based on conditional random fields ( crfs ) and latent-dynamic crfs .
human_abstract: The latent mixture of discriminative experts model is presented in this paper. selected_abstract:
towards assessing changes in degree of depression through facebook
depression is typically diagnosed as being present or absent . however , depression severity is believed to be continuously distributed rather than dichotomous . severity may vary for a given patient daily and seasonally as a function of many variables ranging from life events to environmental factors . repeated population-scale assessment of depression through questionnaires is expensive . in this paper we use survey responses and status updates from 28,749 facebook users to develop a regression model that predicts users degree of depression based on their facebook status updates . our user-level predictive accuracy is modest , significantly outperforming a baseline of average user sentiment . we use our model to estimate user changes in depression across seasons , and find , consistent with literature , users degree of depression most often increases from summer to winter . we then show the potential to study factors driving individuals level of depression by looking at its most highly correlated language features .
human_abstract: In contrast to evaluating depression's presence or absence, this study investigates the use of Facebook data to evaluate ongoing changes in depression severity. The authors use survey data and status updates from 28,749 Facebook users to create a regression model because they understand the expense of conducting numerous, extensive questionnaires. Based on Facebook status updates, this model forecasts a user's level of depression. Even though the predictive accuracy at the user level is low, it performs noticeably better than a baseline based on typical user sentiment. The researchers estimate seasonal changes in depression using their model and find that depression levels typically rise from summer to winter, which is in line with previous research. Finally, by examining the most highly correlated language features in status updates, they show the potential to pinpoint the elements affecting each person's level of depression.

selected_abstract:
combining optimal clustering and hidden markov models for extractive summarization
we propose hidden markov models with unsupervised training for extractive summarization . extractive summarization selects salient sentences from documents to be included in a summary . unsupervised clustering combined with heuristics is a popular approach because no annotated data is required . however , conventional clustering methods such as k-means do not take text cohesion into consideration . probabilistic methods are more rigorous and robust , but they usually require supervised training with annotated data . our method incorporates unsupervised training with clustering , into a probabilistic framework . clustering is done by modified k-means ( mkm ) -- a method that yields more optimal clusters than the conventional k-means method . text cohesion is modeled by the transition probabilities of an hmm , and term distribution is modeled by the emission probabilities . the final decoding process tags sentences in a text with theme class labels . parameter training is carried out by the segmental k-means ( skm ) algorithm . the output of our system can be used to extract salient sentences for summaries, or used for topic detection . content - based evaluation shows that our method outperforms an existing extractive summarizer by 22.8 % in terms of relative similarity, and outperforms a baseline summarizer that selects the top n sentences as salient sentences by 46.3 % .
human_abstract: This study suggests utilising Hidden Markov Models (HMMs) with unsupervised training for extractive summarisation. The goal of extractive summarisation is to create a summary by choosing important sentences from documents. Text cohesion is frequently overlooked by traditional techniques like K-means, despite the fact that unsupervised clustering is popular because it does not require annotation. Although probabilistic approaches are reliable, they usually require supervised training. The authors incorporate clustering and unsupervised training into a probabilistic framework. They use emission probabilities to model term distribution, the HMM's transition probabilities to model text cohesion, and Modified K-Means (MKM) for clustering, which generates more optimal clusters than traditional K-means. Sentences are tagged with theme class labels in the system's final decoding. The Segmental K-Means (SKM) algorithm is used for parameter training. The output can be used to identify topics or extract noteworthy sentences for summaries. This approach significantly outperforms a baseline (top-N sentence selection) by 46.3% and an existing extractive summariser by 22.8% in relative similarity, according to content-based evaluation.
